original_hypothesis.title: Burns Accumulate address(0) in Holders Array Causing Permanent Distribution DoS
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Every burn operation (burn/burnFrom) triggers _beforeTokenTransfer with to=address(0). Since balanceOf(address(0)) is always 0, the check `this.balanceOf(to) != 0` is always false, causing `holders.push(address(0))` on every burn. The _afterTokenTransfer hook only attempts to remove `from` (the burner), never address(0). Over time, the holders array grows unboundedly with address(0) entries. distributeToAllHolders() processes each entry, eventually exceeding block gas limits. Even partial distribute() calls waste gas iterating over phantom entries.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Burns are a public function via ERC20Burnable. Any token holder can burn 1 wei repeatedly to inflate the holders array. The address(0) entries are never cleaned up because _afterTokenTransfer only checks `from`, and no other function removes them.

proof_of_signal.pattern: During burn: to=address(0), balanceOf(address(0))=0 always, so holders.push(address(0)) executes on every burn

preconditions: Attacker holds any amount of LiquidInfrastructureERC20 tokens
burn() is not blocked (within MinDistributionPeriod, not locked)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: to=address(0) during burns always evaluates balanceOf(address(0))==0, triggering holders.push(address(0))
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No guard against pushing address(0); no cleanup mechanism for phantom entries
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing `if (to == address(0)) return;` check before exists check

analysis_questions: How many burns are needed to make distributeToAllHolders() exceed block gas limit?
Can partial distribute() work around the inflated array, or is gas cost per entry too high?
Is there any mechanism to remove address(0) entries from holders?
validation_requirements: Burn tokens N times and verify holders.length increases by N with address(0) entries
Calculate gas per holder entry in distribute() to determine DoS threshold
Verify distributeToAllHolders() reverts with OOG after sufficient burns

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker burns 1 wei per transaction thousands of times. Each burn pushes address(0) to holders. Once holders array contains enough entries (~10K+), distributeToAllHolders() exceeds block gas limit. Even partial distribute() calls spend significant gas on phantom entries. Protocol becomes unable to complete distributions efficiently, potentially permanently.
prerequisites: Token balance (even 1 wei sufficient for many burns)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent DoS on distribution mechanism; all accumulated revenue becomes inaccessible
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All token holders cannot receive distributions
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: No on-chain recovery — no function to clean holders array

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests for holders array state after burns

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: ~50K gas per holder entry in distribute(); block gas limit / 50K = max processable holders

validation_result.mechanism: Unbounded array bloat via address(0) accumulation
validation_result.impact_summary: Permanent DoS of the revenue distribution mechanism and token minting due to block gas limit exhaustion.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The vulnerability stems from the `_beforeTokenTransfer` hook in `LiquidInfrastructureERC20.sol`. During a `burn` or `burnFrom` operation, the `to` address is `address(0)`. The hook checks if the recipient already exists in the `holders` array by evaluating `this.balanceOf(to) != 0`. Since `address(0)` cannot hold a balance, this check always returns `false`, causing `address(0)` to be pushed to the `holders` array on every burn.

The `_afterTokenTransfer` hook only attempts to remove the `from` address (the burner) if their balance reaches 0. It never removes `to` (`address(0)`). Furthermore, an attacker can optimize this attack by holding just 1 wei of the token and repeatedly calling `burn(0)`. Because their balance remains > 0, `stillHolding` evaluates to `true` in `_afterTokenTransfer`, completely bypassing the cleanup loop. This makes the attack O(1) in gas cost (~25k gas per burn), allowing an attacker to add thousands of `address(0)` entries per block.

As the `holders` array grows unboundedly, any function iterating over it will eventually exceed the block gas limit. This permanently breaks `distributeToAllHolders()` and forces users to spend massive amounts of gas calling `distribute(n)` in small batches just to clear phantom entries. Additionally, the `mint` function (which is the only way `address(0)` could theoretically be removed, since `from == address(0)` during minting) will also iterate over the entire bloated array in `_afterTokenTransfer`, causing `mint` to permanently revert with an Out-Of-Gas error.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code evidence perfectly matches the hypothesis. The logic in `_beforeTokenTransfer` unconditionally pushes `address(0)` because its balance is always 0. The lack of cleanup in `_afterTokenTransfer` for the `to` address ensures the entries persist. The inherited `ERC20Burnable` allows any user to call `burn(0)`.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: During a burn, `to` is `address(0)`. `this.balanceOf(address(0))` is always 0, so `exists` is false, and `address(0)` is pushed to the `holders` array.
The cleanup logic only evaluates `from`. If an attacker holds 1 wei and calls `burn(0)`, `stillHolding` is true, bypassing the loop entirely and making the attack extremely cheap.
The distribution loop iterates over all entries in `holders`. Phantom `address(0)` entries force the contract to perform useless SLOADs, wasting gas and eventually causing Out-Of-Gas reverts.

calling_sequence: Cleanup loop is skipped because balanceOf(msg.sender) > 0
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The attacker repeatedly calls `burn(0)`. Each call triggers `_beforeTokenTransfer` which pushes `address(0)` to `holders`. The `_afterTokenTransfer` hook skips cleanup because the attacker maintains a >0 balance. The array grows until `distributeToAllHolders()` and `mint()` exceed the block gas limit.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No config dependencies required for this vulnerability.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The scenario is highly realistic and requires minimal capital. An attacker only needs 1 wei of the token to bypass the O(N) cleanup loop in `_afterTokenTransfer`. By calling `burn(0)` in a loop within a smart contract, they can add thousands of `address(0)` entries per block at a minimal gas cost (~25k gas per entry). Once the array reaches ~15,000 entries, `mint()` will permanently revert with OOG, and `distributeToAllHolders()` will be permanently broken.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL. The attack permanently bricks the protocol's core functionality (revenue distribution and token minting). Accumulated revenue becomes trapped in the contract because the gas cost to distribute it exceeds the block gas limit or the value of the rewards.
recommended_mitigations: Add a guard in `_beforeTokenTransfer` to prevent pushing `address(0)`: `if (to != address(0) && !exists) { holders.push(to); }`
Alternatively, use an EnumerableSet from OpenZeppelin for the `holders` collection to automatically handle uniqueness and O(1) additions/removals.
validation_result.replication_plan:
1. Deploy LiquidInfrastructureERC20.
2. Approve and mint 1 wei to an attacker address.
3. Attacker calls `burn(0)` 100 times in a loop.
4. Verify `holders.length` increases by 100.
5. Attempt to call `distributeToAllHolders()` and observe the massive gas consumption.
